texto: If you wanted to kidnap someone, what would you use?" she asked Amit. They were lying in bed, with the lights off. To knock them unconscious. So that you could drag them into the back of your van."Chloroform, I guess."Really?" She brightened. It made her happy that the person she was marrying would commit crimes in the same way as she would.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Sarah Shun-lien Bynum que provoca temas ligados a amor. Ao questionar “If you wanted to kidnap someone, what would you use?" she asked Amit. They were lying in bed, wit…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre amor, kidnapping, love, com foco em casamento e love.

frase_original: If you wanted to kidnap someone, what would you use?" she asked Amit. They were lying in bed, with the lights off. To knock them unconscious. So that you could drag them into the back of your van."Chloroform, I guess."Really?" She brightened. It made her happy that the person she was marrying would commit crimes in the same way as she would.

texto: But illness does not always write itself upon the body, the sickness I search for is hidden deep within the brain. Sometimes it rises to the surface. Sometimes the face betrays what the body conceals. But there moments, these betrayals, last no longer than an instant. They come, they go, they pass over the patient, darkening and brightening his face like clouds gusting over a meadow. How is it possible, then, to tell what he is suffering when the visible signs of his inner disorder appear so fleetingly upon his face?
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Sarah Shun-lien Bynum: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “But illness does not always write itself upon the body, the sickness I search for is hidden deep …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que tempo, body, conceal pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de dia-chuvoso e reflexao-noturna.

They come, they go, they pass over the patient, darkening and brightening his face like clouds gusting over a meadow. How is it possible, then, to tell what he is suffering when the visible signs of his inner disorder appear so fleetingly upon his face?
